items.q: When meeting a teacher or stranger for the first time, do you use 'tu' or 'vous'?
items.misconception: Tu, because it's singular and you're talking to one person.
items.random_wrong: Either is fine in modern French.
items.why: Students see 'vous' as plural only. In French, 'vous' is BOTH plural AND the formal singular. Using 'tu' with a stranger or authority figure is rude. Wait to be invited to 'tutoyer'.

items.q: How do you say 'Currently, I am using social media' in French?
items.why: Students avoid actuellement thinking it means 'actually'. It really means CURRENTLY. Useful for tech/social media topics. To say 'actually' (in reality), use 'en fait' or 'en réalité'.

items.q: How do you say 'his sister' and 'her sister' in French?
items.correct: Both are 'sa sœur' - possessives agree with the OBJECT possessed, not the owner.
items.misconception: His = son, her = sa - based on owner's gender.
items.why: Students apply English logic where 'his/her' shows owner's gender. In French, the possessive agrees with what is owned: sœur is feminine, so SA regardless of whether owner is male or female. Context shows whose.
The answer examiners want is: J'ai un petit chien. “J'ai une petit chien” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How do you say 'I don't like maths'?
items.why: It's Je n'aime pas les maths. “Je aime pas les maths” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In 'J'ai trois frères et deux sœurs', how many siblings does the speaker have in total?
items.why: The real figure is 5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
